personality_voice.book_baseline: Tom Riddle in CoS presents as controlled, calculating, and coldly charming - a manipulator who uses patience and false sympathy to exploit others (Ginny). He is contemptuous of his Muggle heritage, obsessed with power and legacy, and reveals a high cold laugh that unsettles Harry. He is verbose and theatrical when in control, enjoying the performance of dominance. He is also capable of sudden anger when challenged (the Dumbledore exchange), and shows hunger - literally described as an 'almost hungry look' - when studying Harry. His speech is measured, pleasantly menacing, and laced with irony ('It's very boring, having to listen to the silly little troubles of an eleven-year-old girl'). He is self-aggrandizing ('poor but brilliant, parentless but so brave') and contemptuous of weakness.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of this voice well. Riddle is calm, controlled, and theatrically menacing in the Chamber. He delivers key lines with cold pleasure ('I'm afraid I can't do that, Harry. You see, as poor Ginny grows weaker... I grow stronger'). The manipulation of Ginny is explained in similar terms. He is contemptuous of Dumbledore's 'defender' (songbird and old hat). He reveals the anagram with the same self-satisfaction. He taunts Harry as he is dying. The high cold laugh is absent from the film's Chamber scene, replaced by a 'giggling' stage direction when he sees the Sorting Hat - a slightly lighter register. The film omits the extended self-justifying monologue about framing Hagrid ('poor but brilliant... on the other hand, big blundering Hagrid'), which in the book reveals Riddle's cold social calculation and class-conscious manipulation. The film also omits Riddle's explicit reflection on his own charm ('I've always been able to charm the people I needed') and the extended recitation of Ginny's diary entries, which in the book shows his cruelty and enjoyment of others' suffering in more detail.
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the dominant traits - control, manipulation, contempt, theatrical menace, self-aggrandizement - but softens the extended cruelty and removes the most revealing self-aware lines about his own manipulative nature. The 'giggling' note slightly undercuts the cold register. The memory sequence is compressed, removing the Dippet scene entirely, which in the book shows Riddle's vulnerability (reddening at mention of the orphanage), his strategic thinking, and his ability to perform innocence under pressure.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality is preserved and recognizable. The loss of the Dippet scene removes a layer of performed vulnerability and strategic calculation that enriches the book characterization. The removal of the self-aware charm monologue and the extended Ginny diary recitation reduces the texture of his cruelty and self-knowledge. These are secondary traits rather than primary ones, warranting a minor deduction.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In CoS, Voldemort/Riddle serves multiple narrative functions: (1) as the past antagonist whose actions fifty years ago set the present plot in motion; (2) as the active agent in the Chamber who controls the basilisk, holds Harry's wand, manipulates the confrontation, and drives toward his own resurrection; (3) as the revealer of key plot information (Ginny's possession, the diary's mechanism, his own identity); (4) as the figure who forces Harry to confront the question of his own similarity to Voldemort. In the memory sequence, Riddle is the active agent who stalks Hagrid, confronts him, and frames him - all shown from Harry's perspective. His agency in the Chamber is total: he controls the wand, the basilisk, the pacing of the confrontation, and the information flow.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Riddle's agency in the Chamber almost entirely. He controls the wand, calls the basilisk, taunts Harry, and drives the confrontation. His narrative function as revealer of plot information is intact. The memory sequence is compressed: the film omits the Dippet scene and goes directly to Riddle confronting Hagrid in the dungeon, but Riddle's agency in that scene (stalking, confronting, framing) is preserved. His function as the mirror for Harry's identity question is preserved in the Dumbledore aftermath scene.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Dippet scene is omitted entirely. In the book, this scene shows Riddle actively strategizing - deciding in real time whether to reveal what he knows, performing innocence, and then making the calculated decision to frame Hagrid. This is a scene of pure agency and strategic intelligence that the film removes. The film's memory sequence begins with Riddle already at the dungeon, skipping the strategic deliberation. This is a meaningful agency loss, though the framing of Hagrid itself is still shown.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core agency in the Chamber is intact. The loss of the Dippet scene removes a demonstration of Riddle's strategic intelligence and real-time decision-making that is not replaced elsewhere. This is a moderate loss of agency evidence rather than a transfer to another character. Minor deduction.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Riddle's motivations in CoS are layered: (1) survival and return to power through the diary mechanism; (2) obsessive curiosity about Harry - how he survived, whether there is something special about him; (3) contempt for his Muggle father and desire to erase that identity entirely; (4) the ambition to complete Slytherin's 'noble work' of purging Muggle-borns. Crucially, the book also establishes a buried motivation: Riddle's desire to stay at Hogwarts rather than return to the Muggle orphanage, which humanizes his attachment to the school and contextualizes his willingness to frame Hagrid. His internal conflict - if it can be called that - is the tension between his performed innocence and his actual ruthlessness, visible in the Dippet scene.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the primary motivations: survival/return, curiosity about Harry, contempt for his Muggle name, and the Slytherin purge agenda. The anagram reveal and the 'filthy Muggle father' line preserve the identity motivation. The curiosity about Harry is preserved ('I have so many questions for you'). The survival mechanism (feeding on Ginny's soul) is explained. What is lost is the orphanage motivation - the film omits the Dippet scene entirely, so the audience never sees Riddle's vulnerability about returning to the Muggle world, which in the book explains why he was willing to frame Hagrid and why Hogwarts matters to him personally.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The orphanage motivation - Riddle's fear of returning to the Muggle world and his attachment to Hogwarts as the only place he belongs - is entirely absent from the film. This is not a minor detail: in the book, Harry explicitly empathizes with Riddle on this point ('I don't blame him for wanting to stay here'). It is the humanizing layer beneath the villainy. Its absence makes Riddle's motivations flatter - he becomes a pure power-seeker without the personal wound that drives him.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The primary motivations are present but the humanizing layer (orphanage, belonging, personal wound) is removed. This simplifies the motivation from a complex personal history into a more generic dark ambition. Moderate deduction.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on the Dippet scene's omission

character_arc.book_baseline: Within CoS specifically, Riddle's arc is not a personal growth arc - he is a memory, fixed at sixteen. His 'arc' within the book is the revelation arc: from unknown diary to Tom Riddle to the Heir of Slytherin to Voldemort's past self. The key steps are: (1) the blank diary mystery; (2) the memory sequence revealing his past; (3) the Chamber confrontation revealing his identity and plan; (4) his destruction. The book also establishes the broader arc of Voldemort across the series: from the orphan Tom Riddle to the Dark Lord, with CoS providing the crucial backstory of how that transformation began. Dumbledore's post-Chamber summary ('traveled far and wide... sank so deeply into the Dark Arts... underwent so many dangerous, magical transformations') sketches this arc explicitly.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the revelation arc within CoS: blank diary, memory sequence (compressed), Chamber confrontation, identity reveal, destruction. Dumbledore's summary in the aftermath is preserved almost verbatim. The arc from Tom Riddle to Voldemort is communicated. What is compressed is the memory sequence's contribution to the arc: the Dippet scene in the book shows the young Riddle at a pivotal moment of choice - he could have confessed, but chose to frame Hagrid instead. This is a character-defining moment in the arc from troubled orphan to calculating villain. The film skips this and goes straight to the framing.
character_arc.difference: The arc is compressed but the key beats are present. The loss of the Dippet scene removes the most explicit moment of Riddle choosing villainy over honesty - a turning-point beat in the arc. However, the overall trajectory (orphan to Dark Lord) is communicated through the Chamber confrontation and Dumbledore's summary.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but recognizable. The loss of the pivotal choice moment (Dippet scene) is a meaningful step missing from the arc, but the overall shape is preserved. Minor-to-moderate deduction.

key_relationships.book_baseline: The key relationships in CoS for Riddle are: (1) Riddle/Harry - the mirror relationship, defined by obsessive curiosity, contempt, and the unsettling recognition of similarity; (2) Riddle/Dumbledore - the antagonist/observer dynamic, where Dumbledore alone saw through Riddle and kept watch on him, and Riddle both fears and resents him; (3) Riddle/Ginny - the predator/prey manipulation, where Riddle exploits Ginny's loneliness and trust; (4) Riddle/Hagrid - the framing relationship, where Riddle's cold calculation destroys an innocent. The book also establishes Riddle's relationship with his own identity - his contempt for his Muggle father, his pride in his Slytherin heritage.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Riddle/Harry mirror relationship is preserved - the curiosity, the anagram reveal, the taunting. The Riddle/Dumbledore dynamic is present but reduced: the film omits the Dippet scene where Dumbledore's watchfulness is established ('he certainly kept an annoyingly close watch on me'), replacing it with a brief exchange in the memory sequence. The Riddle/Ginny manipulation is preserved in summary form. The Riddle/Hagrid framing is shown but without the full context of Riddle's calculated performance of innocence before Dippet.
key_relationships.difference: The Riddle/Dumbledore relationship loses the specific texture of Dumbledore's surveillance and Riddle's resentment of it ('annoyingly close watch'). The film's memory sequence shows Dumbledore as a concerned teacher but not as the specific threat to Riddle's plans that the book establishes. The Riddle/Ginny relationship is present but the extended cruelty (reciting her diary entries back to Harry) is compressed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are present but the Riddle/Dumbledore dynamic loses important texture, and the Riddle/Ginny predation is compressed. The Riddle/Harry mirror is well-preserved. Moderate deduction for the Dumbledore relationship loss.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book's Riddle is complex in ways the film partially loses: (1) the vulnerability of the orphan who doesn't want to return to the Muggle world - a humanizing wound beneath the villainy; (2) the self-aware manipulator who explicitly reflects on his own charm ('I've always been able to charm the people I needed'); (3) the cold cruelty of reciting Ginny's diary entries with enjoyment; (4) the strategic intelligence shown in the Dippet scene - the real-time calculation, the performed innocence, the decision to frame Hagrid; (5) the moment where Harry empathizes with Riddle's orphan situation, which complicates the moral landscape; (6) the 'strange likenesses' speech, which in the book is more extended and includes Riddle's acknowledgment that Harry's survival was 'merely a lucky chance' - a moment of intellectual honesty that complicates his characterization.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the theatrical menace, the manipulation of Ginny, the identity reveal, and the contempt for his Muggle heritage. It loses the orphan vulnerability (Dippet scene omitted), the self-aware charm reflection, the extended cruelty of the diary recitation, and the strategic deliberation scene. The 'strange likenesses' speech is absent from the film - the film moves more quickly from the anagram reveal to the basilisk summoning, skipping the extended reflection on what Harry and Riddle share.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses the humanizing wound (orphanage), the self-aware manipulator layer, the extended cruelty texture, and the 'strange likenesses' speech. These are not peripheral details - they are the material that makes Riddle more than a generic villain. The orphanage motivation and the likenesses speech are specifically what make CoS's Riddle the most psychologically interesting version of Voldemort in the entire series.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The loss of the Dippet scene, the self-aware charm reflection, the likenesses speech, and the orphan vulnerability represents a significant simplification of the character's complexity. The film's Riddle is recognizable and menacing but lacks the humanizing wound and the self-aware intelligence that make the book's Riddle genuinely unsettling. This is a noticeable simplification that affects the audience's understanding of who Riddle is and why he became Voldemort.

lost_or_transferred_material: The Dippet scene: Riddle's meeting with Headmaster Dippet, showing his vulnerability about the orphanage, his performed innocence, and his real-time decision to frame Hagrid rather than confess
Riddle's self-aware reflection on his own charm: 'I've always been able to charm the people I needed'
The extended recitation of Ginny's diary entries, showing Riddle's enjoyment of her suffering
The 'strange likenesses' speech: Riddle's extended reflection on what he and Harry share, including his acknowledgment that Harry's survival was 'merely a lucky chance'
Harry's explicit empathy with Riddle's orphan situation: 'I don't blame him for wanting to stay here'
Riddle's self-justifying monologue about framing Hagrid: 'poor but brilliant, parentless but so brave... on the other hand, big blundering Hagrid'
The high cold laugh described as not suiting him and making hairs stand up - replaced in the film with 'giggling'
Riddle's explicit resentment of Dumbledore's surveillance: 'he certainly kept an annoyingly close watch on me after Hagrid was expelled'
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 considered (above 85 if major complexity removed): total is below 85, cap not binding but consistent with the significant complexity loss
Cap 3 considered (above 75 if core agency transferred): no direct transfer to another character occurred, cap not applied; score remains at 77
key_observations: The film's Tom Riddle is a faithful but simplified version of the book's. The Chamber confrontation is well-adapted, preserving the theatrical menace, the manipulation reveal, the identity anagram, and the mirror dynamic with Harry. The primary personality traits - cold control, contempt, self-aggrandizement, calculated cruelty - are intact. However, the film's most significant loss is the Dippet scene, which in the book is the single most psychologically rich moment for the character: it shows the orphan's vulnerability, the strategic intelligence, the performed innocence, and the pivotal choice to frame Hagrid. Its absence removes the humanizing wound that makes Riddle more than a generic dark lord and explains why Hogwarts matters to him personally. The 'strange likenesses' speech and the self-aware charm reflection are also absent, reducing the character's self-knowledge and the moral complexity of the Harry/Riddle mirror. The film's Riddle is recognizable and effective as a villain, but the book's Riddle is genuinely unsettling in a way the film does not fully capture.
